messages.author: Mystic
messages.text: in my case on wio tracker 1110 its not due to anything blocking or bluetooth related (from what i can tell), it's that randomly all subsequent messages will be shifted 4 bytes to the right, meaning they start with null bytes. those shifts add together over time - if i leave it running after the issue first starts then eventually they end up getting shifted 8 bytes, and presumably 12, 16, etc. to me it looks like this shift originates from radiolib, as in nothing in the meshcore firmware is causing that shift, its written by radiolib's readData. the lr1110 does shift the buffer base on each received message, that's mentioned in a comment in radiolib, but as far as i can tell that's handled properly - it first asks the radio for the offset in the rx buffer and the length of the message, then reads from that offset. makes me wonder a little if its an issue with the radio itself, but surely someone would've noticed that. i've just updated my radio from version 0x0307 -> 0x0401, now waiting to see if the issue reoccurs. if it does, i might test this board as a repeater, maybe something companion-specific is triggering this issue somewhere. could be a good idea for others on t1000e to check their radio firmware version and update if possible?

messages.author: Mystic
messages.text: i just had a thought, no idea why this didn't occur to me before. with the issue i'm seeing on my wio tracker 1110 dev board, it's highly likely that repeaters would be fine bc they usually tx after they rx and txing resets the buffer base and the issue clears, so it could well be the same issue. i believe it's not the actual txing that's resetting the buffer base but some change of state that can theoretically be performed after every rx, but that feels really hacky and shouldn't be needed. has anyone with this t1000e deafness on companion been able to view debug logs over serial while the issue is present to confirm if its the same thing i'm seeing?

messages.text: Sorry in advance, probably wasting everyone's time, but I ran the codebase through GPT-5 Codex in VS Code and asked it to analyse the issue with 0x00 being preappended.. If it helps(?) This was the analysis: Why the extra 0x00 shows up When the dispatcher calls _radio->readData(), RadioLib pulls bytes starting from the LR1110’s current FIFO read pointer. If that pointer isn’t at zero, whatever sits there (often the status byte, which is 0x00) becomes the first element we see—hence the prepended zero. In our LR1110 wrapper we never reprogram the buffer base addresses after begin(), and isReceivingPacket() reads/clears IRQ flags before the dispatcher drains the FIFO. Together they let the FIFO pointer stay advanced from the previous frame. So the zero isn’t “added” by MeshCore; it’s the modem returning the byte that lives at the FIFO offset where we started reading. Why a transmit clears it RadioLibWrapper::onSendFinished() calls _radio->finishTransmit(). On LR1110 that routine re-arms the radio for receive, which internally resets the RX base/back into a sane position (and often re-issues SetBufferBaseAddress). With the FIFO pointers realigned, the next readData() begins at offset 0, so the phantom 0x00 vanishes—until the same misaligned receive flow happens again. In short: the “added” zero is the LR1110 handing us the wrong slot because we clear IRQs and never reset the FIFO between receives; finishing a TX implicitly reinitializes the radio, snapping the pointer back to zero. The fix is to explicitly reset RX base addresses (or avoid clearing the IRQ before we drain the FIFO) so every receive starts from buffer index 0 without needing a transmit to recover. Lining up the pieces: this only surfaces after the repeater has been up for a while because the LR1110 keeps walking its RX FIFO pointer forward with every packet, and we never explicitly reset it during a steady-state receive-only session.

messages.author: jgromes
messages.text: I think what happens is that for some reason, the Rx pointer gets moved twice by 8 bytes in total, but the offset reported by the radio is only incremented once by 4 bytes. Almost as if it received two packets at the same time but only managed to process one of them. Of course the interesting question is what is the root cause of this and whether we can do anything about it, it definitely looks like an issue in the radio itself. So you guys can apply some sort of hotfix to get the devices working, while I (try to) get in touch with Semtech.

messages.author: jgromes
messages.text: FWIW I tested out switching Rx to standby to Rx on invalid header IRQ and it seems to fix the issue (fix as in the null bytes will be removed from the subsequent packets). The original packet is lost in this approach though. The switching also seems to improve resilience to the bug - with two ongoing transmissions 60 kHz apart I can trigger the issue pretty reliably (within a couple minutes), but with regular switching it ran for several hours without triggering it.
messages.content_html: FWIW I tested out switching Rx to standby to Rx on invalid header IRQ and it seems to fix the issue (fix as in the null bytes will be removed from the subsequent packets). The original packet is lost in this approach though. The switching also seems to improve resilience to the bug - with two ongoing transmissions 60 kHz apart I can trigger the issue pretty reliably (within a couple minutes), but with regular switching it ran for several hours without triggering it.

messages.author: Mystic
messages.text: basically it adds a u8 counter to the customlr1110 radio class which is the number of bytes shifted when RadioLibWrapper::recvRaw calls getPacketLength on the radio, it calls the override i wrote which calls it on the base class, then if its 0 and header err irq is set it increments the counter by 4 then when recvRaw calls readData on the radio, it calls the override which is basically a copy paste of radiolib's readData but with the counter added to the radio's reported offset in the call to readBuffer8 (reads bytes from the radio's rx buffer) to correct for the shift (edited)
messages.content_html: basically it adds a u8 counter to the customlr1110 radio class which is the number of bytes shifted when RadioLibWrapper::recvRaw calls getPacketLength on the radio, it calls the override i wrote which calls it on the base class, then if its 0 and header err irq is set it increments the counter by 4 then when recvRaw calls readData on the radio, it calls the override which is basically a copy paste of radiolib's readData but with the counter added to the radio's reported offset in the call to readBuffer8 (reads bytes from the radio's rx buffer) to correct for the shift (edited)
